title: How to use your smart meter in-home display to save
description: How to use your smart meter in-home display to cut bills: read the live figure, find your baseload, set budgets and spot waste at 26.11p per kWh.
blurb: Turn the little screen by your kettle into a fault-finder that pays for itself within a week.
lede: Most in-home displays end up face down in a drawer within a month of the smart meter installation, which is a shame, because the little screen is the only tool in your house that shows you what you are spending on energy right now, in pounds, as it happens. Used properly it does two jobs no bill can do: it catches the silent background drain that runs 24 hours a day, and it puts a live price on every appliance you switch on. You do not need an app, an account or any technical knowledge. You need about half an hour, a free evening, and a willingness to walk around the house turning things off while you watch a number fall.
short_answer: Your in-home display shows live usage in watts and pence per hour, plus daily and weekly history. The single most valuable thing it can do is reveal your baseload, the power your home draws when nothing is deliberately on. Every 100 watts of constant background load costs about £229 a year at the current 26.11p per kWh cap rate, and most homes can cut 50 to 150 watts of it in one evening.

sections.h2: What the in-home display actually shows, and what it does not
sections.html:
The in-home display, usually called the IHD, is a small battery-or-mains powered screen your installer hands over with the smart meter. It is not the meter itself. It receives data over a short-range wireless link (the home area network) from the meters, so it works without your broadband and switching it off changes nothing about how you are billed. The page on how smart meters work covers the plumbing behind it.

Almost every model shows the same core information, even though the menus differ by supplier:

  • Live electricity use, in watts or kilowatts, and usually also in pence per hour. This updates every few seconds and is the screen that does the real work.
  • Gas use, updated every 30 minutes rather than live, because the gas meter runs on a battery and only wakes up to transmit twice an hour.
  • Today, this week, this month totals in kWh and pounds, with comparisons against the previous period.
  • A traffic-light indicator (green, amber, red) showing whether your current draw is low, medium or high against your own typical usage.
  • Your tariff rates, pulled from the supplier, which is why the pounds figures are usually accurate without any setup.

What it cannot do is tell you which appliance is using the power. It sees one number for the whole house. To pin a cost on a single device you either use the switch-off method described below, or put a plug-in energy monitor on the suspect for a day or two. The two tools together cover almost every question you will ever have about your electricity use.

sections.h2: Turn the live number into pounds per year
sections.html:
The live screen becomes useful the moment you can translate it. A watt figure means little on its own; the same figure expressed as an annual cost tends to change behaviour on the spot. The conversion is simple: kilowatts times 26.11 gives pence per hour, and anything that runs constantly costs that hourly figure times 8,760 hours a year.

Live readingCost per hourCost per day if constantCost per year if constant
100 W (typical lean baseload)2.6p63p£229
200 W (common baseload)5.2p£1.25£457
500 W (heavy baseload or one old appliance)13.1p£3.13£1,143
1 kW (small heater, dishwasher heating)26.1pn/a, runs in burstsn/a
2 kW (kettle, washing machine heating)52.2pn/a, runs in burstsn/a
3 kW (immersion heater, fan heater on full)78.3pn/a, runs in burstsn/a

At 26.11p per kWh, Ofgem price cap July to September 2026. Checked June 2026.

Two lessons fall straight out of that table. First, the short sharp loads people worry about are mostly cheap: a kettle at 2 kW boiling for two minutes costs under 2p. Second, the loads nobody notices are the expensive ones, because they multiply by 8,760 hours. A forgotten 500 W load somewhere in the house costs more per year than most families spend on their entire electricity standing charge and lighting combined.

sections.h2: The half-hour baseload hunt that pays for itself
sections.html:
This is the highest-value exercise the IHD makes possible, and it only needs doing once or twice a year. Your baseload is what the house draws when nothing is deliberately running: no kettle, no washing, no oven, heating off. It is fridges, routers, standby electronics, chargers, pumps and anything quietly broken. UK homes typically idle between 100 and 300 watts, and the difference between those two figures is about £457 a year at the current cap rate.

Here is the method:

  • Pick a quiet moment. Turn off everything you actively use. Leave the fridge and freezer alone, they are supposed to run.
  • Read the live screen. Wait a minute for it to settle, and note the number. This is your baseload.
  • Walk the house with the display in hand. Most IHDs are portable, and this is exactly what the battery is for. Switch off one suspect at a time at the wall: the TV and soundbar cluster, the games console, the old satellite box, the second router, the desktop PC "asleep" in the spare room, the heated towel rail someone left on in October.
  • Watch the number after each switch. Give it 30 seconds. A drop of 15 watts is £34 a year. A drop of 60 watts, common for an old set-top box plus a console in instant-on mode, is £137 a year.
  • Note what you found and decide what stays off. Anything you only use occasionally goes on a switched extension lead.

The classic culprits, and what to do about each of them long term, are covered in the full guide to standby power. The IHD's job is the hunt; the wins usually come from three or four devices you had genuinely forgotten existed.

The same trick catches faults. A fridge with a failed door seal or a frosted-up freezer shows as a baseload that has crept up since you last checked. A central heating pump running constantly in summer shows up the same way. People regularly find faults this way that would otherwise have sat on the bill for years.

sections.h2: Use the history and budget screens, not just the live one
sections.html:
The live screen finds waste; the history screens stop it coming back. Three habits are worth building:

Compare like with like, weekly

Glance at "this week versus last week" once a week. The point is not the absolute figure, it is the trend. If the weather has not changed but the number has jumped 20 per cent, something in the house changed and it is worth a five-minute investigation while the cause is fresh.

Set the budget alert if your display has one

Most IHDs let you set a daily or weekly spending target and will flag when you are on course to miss it. Set it slightly below your current typical day rather than at some aspirational figure. A target you beat most days keeps you looking at the screen; one you blow through by lunchtime gets the display put in the drawer.

Learn your gas shape in winter

The gas figure only updates every half hour, but the daily totals are gold in the heating season. Gas at 7.33p per kWh is where most of a typical home's energy money actually goes, and the IHD lets you see, the very next morning, what an hour less heating or a one-degree lower thermostat did to the day's total. That fast feedback loop is exactly what experiments like timed heating bursts versus leaving it on all day need to be worth running in your own house rather than arguing about in the abstract.

sections.h2: What the display tells you about your tariff
sections.html:
Because the IHD holds your actual unit rates and standing charges, it doubles as a sanity check on your bill. Two things are worth looking at deliberately.

First, the standing charge. Many displays show cost from midnight, so the day starts at around 86p (57.19p electricity plus 29.04p gas on a typical dual-fuel setup) before you have used anything. That is not an error. It is the fixed daily fee explained in the standing charges guide, and seeing it on the screen every morning is a useful reminder of how much of a small household's bill is fixed rather than usage.

Second, the rates themselves. Check the unit rate shown on the display against your latest bill or your online account. If they disagree, the display is usually the stale one and a supplier refresh fixes it, but occasionally it is the bill that is wrong, and the display is how people notice. The guide to reading your energy bill shows where to find the official figures to compare against.

If you are on a time-of-use tariff, or thinking about one, the IHD earns its keep again. On Economy 7 and similar tariffs the display shows which rate is active, which makes it much easier to build the habit of pushing the dishwasher, washing machine and EV charging into the cheap window. And a few weeks of half-hourly history is exactly the evidence you need to judge whether a time-of-use tariff would suit your household before you commit.

sections.h2: When the display is blank, wrong or missing
sections.html:
A few practical points, because a dead IHD is the most common reason people give up on the whole idea:

  • No display, or it never worked? Suppliers must offer you an IHD when the smart meter is installed, and they are responsible for fixing or replacing one that fails within the first 12 months. After that, policies vary; ask anyway, and check the current rules on the Ofgem or Smart Energy GB pages since obligations change. Many suppliers will also point you to their app, which shows the same data and sometimes more.
  • Display lost connection? The wireless link to the meter has limited range, roughly within a normal house. If you moved the display to a far bedroom or the meter is in an outbuilding, bring it closer and let it sit for an hour. A restart (hold the power button, or remove power for a minute) fixes most of the rest.
  • Pounds figures look wrong? Check the rates in the settings menu against your bill, as above. After a tariff change it can take a day or two for new rates to reach the display.
  • Switched supplier and it went dumb? This mostly affected first-generation (SMETS1) meters and has largely been fixed by remote enrolment, but if your meter stopped sending readings after a switch, tell the new supplier, and keep submitting manual meter readings until it is sorted so your bills stay accurate.

None of this costs you anything. The display itself draws under a watt, around £1 to £2 a year if left plugged in, and there is no subscription. It is one of the very few energy gadgets with no catch.

An in-home display will not save you money by sitting on the kitchen counter showing green. It saves money when you use it three ways: one deliberate baseload hunt to strip out the constant background waste, which is typically worth £50 to £250 a year for half an hour's work; a weekly glance at the history screens to catch creep and faults early; and as live feedback when you test a change, whether that is a lower boiler flow temperature, a shorter shower or shifting loads into a cheap-rate window. The screen is free, the data is yours, and the expensive watts are the ones running while you sleep. Go and find them.

faq.q: Does the in-home display itself cost money to run?
faq.a: Almost nothing. A typical IHD draws under one watt, which is around £1 to £2 a year at current rates if you leave it plugged in. There is no subscription and it uses its own wireless link to the meter, not your broadband.

faq.q: Is the in-home display the same thing as the smart meter?
faq.a: No. The meters are the boxes that measure and report your usage for billing. The display is just a portable screen that receives their data. Unplugging or losing the display has no effect on your meter, your readings or your bill.

faq.q: Why does my gas reading only update every half hour?
faq.a: The gas meter runs on a long-life battery, so to conserve it the meter only wakes and transmits every 30 minutes. Electricity is mains powered, which is why that figure updates every few seconds.

faq.q: My display has stopped working. Can I get a free replacement?
faq.a: Your supplier must offer an IHD at installation and is responsible for one that fails within the first 12 months. After that it varies by supplier, so ask, and check the current rules on the Ofgem or Smart Energy GB websites. Most suppliers also offer an app showing the same data.

faq.q: What is a normal baseload for a UK home?
faq.a: Roughly 100 to 300 watts when nothing is deliberately on, covering fridges, the router and standby devices. At 26.11p per kWh every constant 100 watts costs about £229 a year, so it pays to get toward the bottom of that range.
